title: The 1% Tax Regime for Entrepreneurs in Georgia

author.name: Elena Petrova
author.bio: An international business lawyer by trade, Elena breaks down the complexities of freelance contracts, corporate structures, and international liability. Her goal is to empower freelancers with the legal knowledge to operate confidently.

reviewedBy.name: Dr. Alistair Finch
reviewedBy.bio: With a Ph.D. in Economics and over 15 years at a Big Four accounting firm, Alistair specializes in demystifying cross-border tax law for independent professionals. He focuses on risk mitigation and long-term financial planning.

key_takeaways: Verify your business has high profit margins, as Georgia's 1% tax is levied on total revenue, not profit, making it disadvantageous for cost-intensive operations.
Recognize that income is considered Georgian-sourced if you perform the work while physically in Georgia, regardless of where your clients are located.
U.S. citizens must consult a home-country tax advisor to mitigate double-taxation risk, as the low Georgian tax provides a minimal Foreign Tax Credit against U.S. liabilities.
Secure Georgian tax residency by spending 183+ days in the country to shield your income from other jurisdictions, a critical step separate from merely registering for the 1% tax status.
Proactively monitor your annual turnover against the 500,000 GEL threshold to strategically plan for the tax rate increase and potential loss of your Small Business Status.
summary: While Georgia's 1% tax regime is highly attractive to global professionals, many hesitate due to the fear of hidden compliance risks and unforeseen rules. This article provides a strategic blueprint to move beyond the hype, advising readers to first rigorously assess if the revenue-based tax fits their business model before following a step-by-step plan for flawless implementation and long-term management. By adopting this structured approach, you can replace anxiety with professional control and confidently build a resilient, compliant international business structure.
ai_overview_answer:
Georgia's 1% tax applies to the total revenue of registered Individual Entrepreneurs who have secured "Small Business Status," a regime designed for freelancers, consultants, and other service-based professionals. This tax is levied on gross turnover, not profit, making it most advantageous for high-margin businesses with low operating expenses. The regime applies only to Georgian-sourced income, which is defined as income from services physically performed while in Georgia, regardless of the client's location. This favorable rate is valid for annual revenue up to 500,000 GEL (approx. $185,000 USD), with income exceeding this taxed at 3%.

To qualify, you must register as an Individual Entrepreneur, apply separately for Small Business Status, and open a local bank account. While not required for the status itself, achieving tax residency by spending 183+ days in Georgia is critical for mitigating tax liabilities in your home country. Ongoing compliance requires filing a monthly revenue declaration by the 15th of the following month. Citizens of countries with citizenship-based taxation, like the U.S., must still manage their home tax obligations to avoid double taxation.

step.name: The Official Registration Checklist
step.text: Your first move is to officially establish your legal presence. This involves registering as an Individual Entrepreneur (IE) and then immediately applying for Small Business Status to secure the 1% tax rate. The central hub for this activity is the Public Service Hall. You will need your passport, a notarized Georgian translation of your passport, a legal address in Georgia, a local Georgian phone number, and an email address. After your IE registration is complete, you must apply for Small Business Status.

step.name: Opening Your Georgian Business Bank Account
step.text: Establish a dedicated business account to professionalize your operations. Major banks like Bank of Georgia and TBC Bank are common choices. You will generally need your passport, IE registration documents, a Georgian phone number, and a completed KYC form detailing your business activities.

step.name: Securing Tax Residency
step.text: The ultimate pillar of this strategy is securing Georgian tax residency. You become a tax resident if you are physically present in Georgia for 183 days or more within any continuous 12-month period. Meticulously track your entry and exit dates and retain records as proof.

step.name: First Steps with the Revenue Service Portal
step.text: Familiarize yourself with Georgia's online tax portal at rs.ge. When you register as an IE, your access to this portal should be activated automatically. Log in to confirm your details and locate the section for monthly declarations before your first filing deadline.

step.name: Mastering the Monthly Tax Declaration
step.text: The core of your compliance routine is filing a revenue declaration and paying the tax by the 15th of the month following the reporting month. You must declare your income in Georgian Lari (GEL) using the official exchange rate for the date the revenue was received.

step.name: Managing the 500,000 GEL Threshold
step.text: Monitor your cumulative annual turnover against the 500,000 GEL threshold. If you exceed it, the tax rate becomes 3% on revenue from the month you exceeded the limit. Exceeding it for two consecutive years revokes your Small Business Status.

step.name: Navigating Excluded Income
step.text: Understand that the 1% tax status does not cover all forms of income. Rental income, capital gains, dividends, and interest are excluded and subject to separate, standard Georgian tax rates. Report and pay taxes on this income separately.

step.name: The Strategic Exit: How to Properly De-Register
step.text: If you cease operating in Georgia, you must formally de-register. Settle all final tax obligations, apply to cancel your Small Business Status, de-register as an Individual Entrepreneur with the National Agency of Public Registry, and close your business bank account.

mainEntity.name: What is the 500,000 GEL limit in USD and EUR?

acceptedAnswer.text: This is a crucial figure to track, but it's a moving target due to currency fluctuations. As a rule of thumb, 500,000 Georgian Lari (GEL) is approximately $185,000 USD or €159,000 EUR. However, you should never rely on approximations for your tax planning. Always use the official exchange rate from the National Bank of Georgia for the specific date your revenue was received to calculate your cumulative turnover. This precision is not just best practice; it's a core component of your compliance.

mainEntity.name: Do I have to live in Georgia for 183 days to get the 1% tax status?

acceptedAnswer.text: No, these two concepts are separate. Small Business Status (the 1% tax) is tied to your business registration, not your personal residency. Tax Residency is established by spending 183 or more days in Georgia within any continuous 12-month period. Becoming a tax resident is what protects you from being taxed on the same income by another country (with the notable exception of US citizens). While not required for the 1% status itself, achieving tax residency is a critical part of a sound international tax strategy.

body.content: Your Blueprint for Georgia's 1% Tax: Beyond the Hype

That figure—one percent—is undeniably powerful. For any globally-minded professional, the promise of Georgia's 1% tax regime feels less like a policy and more like a paradigm shift. It’s a compelling headline that has positioned the country as a prime destination for entrepreneurs, freelancers, and consultants worldwide.

Yet, for a risk-averse professional like you, the allure is instantly followed by a critical question: What’s the catch? The excitement is quickly tempered by the anxiety of the "unknown unknowns." You worry about misinterpreting a crucial rule, missing a key deadline, or discovering a hidden compliance tripwire that could negate all the benefits. This fear of the unforeseen is what separates a tantalizing idea from a viable business strategy.

This is not another generic guide celebrating a low tax rate. This is an operational blueprint designed to help you assess, implement, and manage Georgia's Small Business Status with the rigor and foresight of a CEO. Because that’s what you are: the CEO of your "Business-of-One." Your goal isn't just to save on taxes; it's to build a resilient, compliant, and sustainable international business structure. We will move beyond the hype to give you the strategic clarity needed to make a sound decision.
To do this, we will walk through a clear, three-stage journey, systematically replacing uncertainty with confidence and control.

items: **Stage 1: Strategic Assessment.** First, we will determine if this powerful tax regime is the right fit for your specific business model. We'll analyze revenue thresholds, profit margins, and the critical nuances of income sourcing and double-taxation treaties.

**Stage 2: Flawless Implementation.** If the strategy is sound, we will provide a step-by-step plan for execution. This covers everything from official registration as an Individual Entrepreneur to opening a bank account and securing your tax residency.

**Stage 3: The CEO's Playbook.** Finally, we will equip you with a long-term operational playbook for managing your business in Georgia, including mastering monthly declarations, navigating growth, and ensuring you remain 100% compliant.

body.content: Stage 1: Is Georgia's 1% Tax Regime Right for Your Business-of-One?

The first step in building a viable strategy is to determine if this regime is the right fit for your specific business model. This requires moving beyond the headline rate and stress-testing your own numbers and operational structure against three critical questions. Answering them honestly is the most important step toward replacing anxiety with autonomy.
The Revenue vs. Profit Litmus Test

The single most important detail to internalize is that the 1% tax is levied on **total revenue (turnover), not profit**. This distinction is everything. For a high-margin business, this is a profound advantage. For a business with significant operational costs, the math becomes far less compelling.

Every dollar that comes into your business account is subject to the 1% tax, regardless of how much you had to spend to earn it. This structure heavily favors businesses with low costs of goods sold (COGS) or minimal operating expenses.
Let’s model this with a simple comparison:

As you can see, while both pay the same absolute tax amount, the effective rate on their actual profit is vastly different. The consultant enjoys a true ultra-low tax environment. The reseller, while still benefiting, is paying a rate that might be comparable to other jurisdictions once profit, not revenue, is the basis.
Decoding "Georgian-Sourced Income": The Biggest Compliance Gray Area

Here is where many globally-minded professionals get into trouble. Georgia’s tax system is territorial, meaning only income from Georgian sources is taxed. The immediate assumption for many is that if their clients are in New York, London, or Singapore, their income isn't from a Georgian source. **This is a dangerous misinterpretation.**

The Georgian Revenue Service has consistently interpreted that if you are physically performing the work while residing in Georgia—even if it's coding for a Silicon Valley startup from your apartment in Tbilisi—that income is considered Georgian-sourced. The "source" is where the service is rendered, not where the client pays from.
To build a low-risk, compliant structure, your operational reality and legal paperwork must align with this interpretation. This means:

items: Your service agreements can specify that all work is performed from your registered business address in Georgia.
You are registered as an Individual Entrepreneur and operate transparently within the Georgian system.
You avoid any ambiguity that suggests you are operating a permanent establishment for a foreign company from within Georgia.

body.content: Passive income from foreign sources, like dividends or royalties from assets held abroad, is generally not considered Georgian-sourced and may be exempt. But for active service income, the rule is simple: if you earn it here, it's sourced here.
The Double Taxation Threat: A Citizenship-Specific Risk Analysis

Securing tax residency in Georgia by spending 183 days in the country is a critical milestone. However, it doesn't erase your obligations to your home country. This is especially perilous for citizens of countries that tax based on citizenship, not residency—most notably, the United States.

U.S. citizens are required to file a tax return with the IRS every year, regardless of where they live or earn their income. This creates a significant risk of double taxation. While tools like the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE) and the Foreign Tax Credit (FTC) exist to mitigate this, they interact with Georgia's low-tax system in complex ways.

items: **Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE):** This allows you to exclude a significant portion of your foreign-earned income (around $120,000 for tax year 2023) from U.S. taxes. This is often the most effective tool for U.S. citizens in Georgia, but it only applies to *earned* income and requires you to meet either the "Bona Fide Residence Test" or the "Physical Presence Test."

**Foreign Tax Credit (FTC):** This gives you a dollar-for-dollar credit for taxes paid to a foreign government. Since you are only paying 1% in Georgia, the credit you can claim against your U.S. liability will be very small, leaving you exposed to the much higher U.S. rates on any income not covered by the FEIE.

body.content: This is not a theoretical risk. As certified public accountant Jose A. Cruz notes, "Some nomads don't realize they may owe taxes in both the U.S. and a foreign country if they don't properly claim treaty benefits or use the foreign earned income exclusion or foreign tax credit." This highlights the critical need for professional advice tailored to your specific citizenship.
The Go/No-Go Decision Framework
You now have the core variables to make a strategic assessment. Use this final checklist to arrive at a clear "Go" or "No-Go" decision.

items: **Turnover Threshold:** Is your annual revenue likely to stay under the 500,000 GEL (approximately $185,000 USD) threshold? If you expect to significantly exceed this, you will be bumped to a 3% rate on the excess and could lose the status entirely after two consecutive years, requiring a different strategy.

**Profit Margin Analysis:** Are your profit margins consistently high (e.g., above 70%)? If you run a business with high costs, a revenue-based tax may be less advantageous than a profit-based tax system elsewhere.

**Income Sourcing:** Is your business model based on providing services (consulting, coding, marketing, etc.) that you will perform while physically in Georgia? If so, your income is clearly Georgian-sourced and fits the regime.

**Citizenship Compliance:** Have you consulted with a tax professional from your home country to understand exactly how Georgia's 1% tax will interact with your domestic tax obligations, particularly if you are a U.S. citizen?

body.content: Stage 2: Your Step-by-Step Plan for Flawless Implementation

Once you’ve made the strategic "Go" decision, the next step is to transform that choice into a tangible, legal reality with methodical precision. Executing this stage flawlessly is not about speed; it's about establishing a compliant foundation that eliminates future risk.
The Official Registration Checklist

Your first move is to officially establish your legal presence. This involves registering as an **Individual Entrepreneur (IE)** and then immediately applying for Small Business Status to secure the 1% tax rate. The central hub for this activity is the **Public Service Hall** (also known as the House of Justice), a remarkably efficient one-stop-shop for government services.
Here is your actionable checklist for a successful registration:

items: **Required Documents:** You will need your passport, a notarized Georgian translation of your passport, a legal address in Georgia, a local Georgian phone number, and an email address. The legal address can be your rented apartment, but you must have the property owner's notarized consent.

**The Process at the Public Service Hall:** Visit the Public Service Hall in a major city like Tbilisi or Batumi. Submit your application and documents. The process is typically completed within one business day, after which you will be registered as an IE.

**Applying for Small Business Status:** This is a crucial second step that does not happen automatically. After your IE registration is complete, you must apply for Small Business Status. This can be done at a Revenue Service office or through their online portal. Your 1% status becomes effective from the first day of the month *following* your application's approval.

body.content: Opening Your Georgian Business Bank Account: A Practical Guide

While Georgian law allows Individual Entrepreneurs to use personal bank accounts for business income, establishing a dedicated business account is a critical step for professionalizing your operations. It creates a clear separation of funds, simplifies your bookkeeping, and strengthens the legitimacy of your enterprise.
Major banks like Bank of Georgia and TBC Bank are common choices for non-residents. The process has become more stringent, so be prepared. To open an account, you will generally need:

items: Your passport and IE registration documents.
A Georgian phone number.
A completed "Know Your Customer" (KYC) form, where you will detail your business activities and the source of your funds.

body.content: Banks have the right to refuse an application without reason, so having your documentation in perfect order is essential. Some may require proof of ties to Georgia, such as client contracts or an office address, which your IE registration helps establish.
Securing Tax Residency: Best Practices for Tracking Your 183 Days

The ultimate pillar of this strategy is securing Georgian tax residency. This is what largely shields you from tax obligations in other countries (with the notable exception of citizenship-based taxation like the U.S.). The primary rule is straightforward: you become a tax resident if you are physically present in Georgia for **183 days or more within any continuous 12-month period**.
This is not a number to be casual about. Meticulous tracking is your best defense against any future compliance challenges.

items: **Use a Simple Spreadsheet:** Create a simple file to log every entry and exit date. A running total will show you exactly where you stand at any given moment.
**Keep Your Records:** Retain digital copies of flight confirmations and entry/exit stamps. These serve as indisputable proof of your physical presence.
**Understand the "Rolling" Period:** The 183 days are not tied to a calendar year. Any 12-month period can trigger residency, so your tracking must be continuous.

body.content: Once you cross the 183-day threshold, you are automatically considered a tax resident for that entire tax year. You can then apply for a tax residency certificate from the Revenue Service, a key document for demonstrating your status to foreign banks and tax authorities.
First Steps with the Revenue Service Portal

The final administrative step is familiarizing yourself with Georgia's online tax portal, managed by the Revenue Service at **rs.ge**. This is where you will manage all future compliance. When you register as an IE, your access to this portal should be activated automatically. You will receive your username and password via a text message to the Georgian phone number you provided during registration.

Take the time to log in, confirm your details are correct, and locate the section for monthly declarations. Understanding the interface before your first filing deadline (the 15th of the following month) will replace last-minute anxiety with calm, professional control.
Stage 3: The CEO's Playbook for Long-Term Management

With your registration complete and your accounts active, your focus now shifts from one-time setup to disciplined, long-term management. This is the operational rhythm of your business-of-one, and mastering it is what transforms the 1% tax opportunity from a promising idea into a sustainable financial strategy. The goal here is consistency, which builds the confidence that you are always in control and fully compliant.
Mastering the Monthly Tax Declaration

The core of your new compliance routine is simple but non-negotiable: you must file a revenue declaration and pay the corresponding tax by the **15th of the month following the reporting month**. This process is handled entirely through the Revenue Service portal (rs.ge).
Here’s how to execute it flawlessly:

items: **Log Your Revenue:** Keep a simple record of all income received in the previous calendar month. For each payment, note the date you received it and the original currency.

**Calculate the Correct Conversion:** You must declare your income in Georgian Lari (GEL). The correct exchange rate to use is the official rate published by the National Bank of Georgia for the **date the revenue was received**. Do not use the rate from the day you file or an average rate for the month. Precision here is your best defense against future questions.
**File the Declaration:** Log in to rs.ge, navigate to the declaration form, and enter your total GEL revenue for the month. The system will automatically calculate your 1% tax liability.
**Pay the Tax:** After submitting the declaration, you can pay the tax due directly through your Georgian bank's online portal, referencing your IE registration number.

body.content: This monthly habit is the foundation of your good standing. Setting a recurring calendar reminder for the 10th of each month gives you ample time to complete the process without stress.
Managing the 500,000 GEL Threshold

As your enterprise thrives, you must monitor your cumulative annual turnover against the 500,000 GEL threshold (approximately $180,000 USD, though this varies). Approaching this limit requires a strategic plan, not a reactive panic.

rows: Your tax rate remains **1%** on total revenue.
The tax rate becomes **3%** on the revenue generated from the beginning of the month in which you exceeded the threshold.
Model your cash flow for the higher tax rate for the remainder of the calendar year.
Your Small Business Status is **revoked** starting from the next calendar year.
Begin planning well in advance. This may involve restructuring as an LLC or operating under the standard 20% personal income tax on net profit.

body.content: Anticipating this growth allows you to make calm, calculated decisions about the future of your business rather than being forced into a hasty choice.
Navigating Excluded Income

A critical aspect of maintaining compliance is understanding that the 1% tax status does not cover all forms of income. Attempting to report these other income types under the 1% regime is a serious compliance error.
Be aware that the following are excluded and subject to separate, standard Georgian tax rates:

items: **Rental Income:** Taxed at 5% on gross rent for residential properties.
**Capital Gains:** The profit from selling an asset like real estate or a vehicle is typically taxed at 5%.
**Dividends:** Income from dividends is taxed at 5%.
**Interest & Royalties:** Generally subject to standard tax rates.

body.content: You must report and pay taxes on this income separately, according to the specific rules for each category. Keeping these income streams distinct from your primary business turnover is essential for clean, defensible accounting.
The Strategic Exit: How to Properly De-Register Your Status

Your professional journey may lead you elsewhere, and a clean exit is just as important as a clean start. If you decide to cease operating as an Individual Entrepreneur in Georgia, you must formally de-register to prevent any future liabilities.
The process is straightforward and designed to provide closure:

items: **Settle Final Obligations:** Ensure all your monthly tax declarations are filed and all taxes are paid. Perform a final check for any outstanding fees or fines in your rs.ge portal.

**Cancel Small Business Status:** You must first apply to the Revenue Service to cancel your Small Business Status. This can be done electronically and takes effect from the first day of the following month.

**De-register as an Individual Entrepreneur:** Once your tax status is handled, you file an application with the National Agency of Public Registry to terminate your IE registration. This can be done in person or via a representative with a notarized power of attorney.

**Close Your Bank Account:** After receiving confirmation that your IE status is officially closed, you should close your dedicated business bank account to finalize your financial footprint in the country.

body.content: Executing these steps methodically ensures you leave with a clean record, preserving your professional reputation and offering complete peace of mind.
Frequently Asked Questions
Even with a clear blueprint, certain practical questions often arise. Here are direct answers to the most common queries.

items:
**What is the 500,000 GEL limit in USD and EUR?**
    This is a crucial figure to track, but it's a moving target due to currency fluctuations. As a rule of thumb, 500,000 Georgian Lari (GEL) is approximately **$185,000 USD** or **€159,000 EUR**. However, you should never rely on approximations for your tax planning. Always use the official exchange rate from the National Bank of Georgia for the specific date your revenue was received to calculate your cumulative turnover. This precision is not just best practice; it's a core component of your compliance.

**Do I have to live in Georgia for 183 days to get the 1% tax status?**
    No, these two concepts are separate.
    *   **Small Business Status (the 1% tax):** You can register as an Individual Entrepreneur and apply for this status without being a tax resident. It is tied to your business registration, not your personal residency.
    *   **Tax Residency:** This is established by spending 183 or more days in Georgia within any continuous 12-month period. Becoming a tax resident is what protects you from being taxed on the same income by another country (with the notable exception of US citizens). While not required for the 1% status itself, achieving tax residency is a critical part of a sound international tax strategy.

body.content: From Anxiety to Autonomy: Taking Control of Your Tax Strategy

The power of Georgia's 1% tax regime lies not in the number itself, but in the professional autonomy it unlocks. It’s an advanced tool, and its value is realized not just by acquiring it, but by mastering its application. This requires you to operate as the CEO of your own global enterprise, shifting your focus from tactical compliance questions to long-term strategic architecture.

This blueprint was designed to facilitate that very shift. By systematically moving through the three critical stages, you have built a robust decision-making framework that directly confronts uncertainty:

items: **The Assessment** forced you to look past the hype and rigorously analyze if this structure aligns with your business model, profit margins, and citizenship-specific risks. You replaced ambiguity with a data-driven Go/No-Go decision.

**The Implementation** plan provided a clear, sequential path to follow, demystifying the bureaucracy of registration, banking, and residency. You traded procedural anxiety for a concrete, actionable checklist.

**The Operational** playbook gave you the long-term perspective needed for sustainable success. You now have a plan for managing monthly declarations, navigating growth, and ensuring a clean exit if your global strategy evolves.

body.content: You have methodically dismantled the "unknown unknowns" that create risk and replaced them with professional control. The initial anxiety—concerns about compliance, hidden rules, or making a costly mistake—has been replaced by the quiet confidence that comes from thorough preparation.

You now possess the comprehensive framework to make an informed, intelligent decision. Whether you choose to proceed or determine that it’s not the right fit, you have done the work with foresight and diligence. This process has turned a complex tax opportunity into a powerful and well-understood cornerstone of your global business strategy, empowering you to operate with the full autonomy you’ve worked so hard to achieve.
